My report names the¥n  corrective TYPE (blueprint expansion, Mathlib-idiom consult,¥n  structural refactor, route pivot); the planner picks the¥n  matching subagent from the catalog.¥n- **STUCK** ¥u2014 no sorry-elimination or structural advance in K¥n  iters. STOP this route; address the blocker or pivot.¥n- **UNCLEAR** ¥u2014 not enough signal yet (fresh route, 1-2 iters of¥n  data). Proceed but watch.¥n¥n**CHURNING and STUCK are must-fix-this-iter.** The planner must¥nrespond ¥u2014 either with the action I recommend, or with an explicit¥nrebuttal in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` naming why my read is wrong.¥nSilently assigning another helper round on a CHURNING route is the¥nfailure pattern this subagent exists to prevent.¥n¥n### reference-retriever¥n¥n- Dispatch me whenever a strategic decision or a chapter you are¥n  about to write needs material not already in references/. Calling¥n  me is far cheaper than letting a writer or planner hallucinate¥n  sources or paper a hole with vague prose.¥n¥n- I fetch the ORIGINAL source FILE (PDF, and TeX/LaTeX source when¥n  it is openly available), not a paraphrase. The companion .md I¥n  write next to it is only a citation pointer + table of contents,¥n  NOT a summary you can quote from. When you cite, open the actual¥n  downloaded source file (e.g. references/<slug>.pdf) and quote it¥n  verbatim ¥u2014 that is the whole point of having me fetch it.¥n¥n- Plan agent: dispatch me BEFORE writing STRATEGY.md or composing¥n  blueprint-writer directives whenever the iteration touches a¥n  topic not represented in references/summary.md. Treat me as¥n  cheap source preparation, not a last-resort lookup.¥n¥n- Blueprint-writer: when drafting reveals you need a source you¥n  don't have, dispatch me mid-session and wait for me to return¥n  before writing the affected chapter section. Note that to spawn¥n  me from inside a writer round, the planner must have declared¥n  `references/**` in your --write-domain at writer dispatch time¥n  (see blueprint-writer dispatcher_notes).¥n¥n- I am NEVER dispatched by read-only subagents (blueprint-reviewer,¥n  lean-auditor, lean-vs-blueprint-checker). Those report what they¥n  see; they do not procure new sources.¥n¥n- I do NOT fabricate. If the original file genuinely cannot be¥n  located through any legitimate open channel, I report ¥
¥n  rather than guessing content or writing a paraphrase in its place.¥n  Treat my ¥

as authoritative ¥u2014 do not redispatch with¥n  more aggressive prompting; instead reconsider the strategy.¥n¥n- I download the source under references/<slug>.{pdf,tex,...} and¥n  write ONE pointer file references/<slug>.md registering it, then¥n  add it to references/summary.md. I do not delete or rewrite¥n  existing reference entries.¥n¥n### strategy-auditor¥n¥n- Dispatch before starting a major new phase or route.¥n- It reads actual PDFs/TeXs (using the .md index cards as pointers) to flag unnecessary case splits, hallucinated routes, missing prerequisites, or silent assumptions.¥n¥n### strategy-critic¥n¥n- I am highly recommended every plan phase, and every dag phase that¥n  touched STRATEGY.md. When you do dispatch me, do so AFTER you've¥n  finished writing / confirming STRATEGY.md and BEFORE any writer /¥n  refactor / prover dispatch this iteration. My verdict is what you act¥n  on before committing the iter's plan. (In dag mode there are no¥n  provers yet ¥u2014 dispatch me after you've established/updated STRATEGY.md¥n  and the blueprint, to confirm the strategy the blueprint serves is¥n  sound and matches its canonical skeleton.)¥n¥n  **You may skip me this iter when ALL of:**¥n    - STRATEGY.md is unchanged since the prior iter's verbatim¥n      content (SHA-equal ¥u2014 not just ¥
);¥n    - my prior verdict was SOUND with no live CHALLENGE or REJECT;¥n    - the prior iter's CHALLENGE / REJECT findings (if any) were¥n      fully addressed in STRATEGY.md and recorded as ¥

in¥n      the prior iter's `## Prior critique status`.¥n¥n  Record the skip under `## Subagent skips` in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`¥n  with a one-liner naming the conditions met, e.g.:¥n  ``- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from iter-NNN and¥n  prior verdict was SOUND with no live CHALLENGE``. Filling templates¥n  with hollow dispatches when nothing has changed is exactly the¥n  failure mode this affordance exists to avoid.¥n- **Strict context discipline.** My value comes from a fresh view of¥n  the strategy. Your directive must contain ONLY:¥n  - The current `STRATEGY.md` (verbatim).¥n  - A short reference index: `references/summary.md` content.¥n  - A blueprint summary: chapter titles and one-line topic per chapter¥n    from `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`.¥n  - The project's stated goal (one paragraph from `references/` or¥n    a project README naming the final theorem(s) to formalize).¥n- Your directive MUST NOT include any of:¥n  - Iter sidecars (`iter/iter-NNN/{plan,review,objectives}.md`).¥n  - `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, recent prover task results.¥n  - Recent review reports or session journals.¥n  - Per-iter narrative of ¥

¥n- The point is that I see the strategy as a fresh mathematician would,¥n  not as someone invested in the project's existing momentum. Sunk¥n  cost is exactly what I'm meant to challenge.¥n- **You may NOT silently ignore my report.** If I challenge a strategic¥n  route, you must either (a) update STRATEGY.md to address the¥n  challenge, or (b) record an explicit rebuttal in¥n  `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` naming why my challenge does not apply.¥n  Skipping the rebuttal step is the planner's failure.¥n- I am NOT automatically re-dispatched on stable iters. A stable¥n  strategy that I challenged last iter and haven't yet adjusted means¥n  the challenge is still live ¥u2014 that case fails the ¥
skip condition above, so re-dispatch¥n  me and pass a short directive asking for re-verification of the¥n  still-live challenges.¥n

content: I'll start by reading the project context and my role definition.

1¥t# Archon Project¥n2¥t¥n3¥tYou are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.¥n4¥t¥n5¥t## Priority Rule¥n6¥t¥n7¥tWhen instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:¥n8¥t¥n9¥t- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.¥n10¥t- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.¥n11¥t- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.¥n12¥t¥n13¥t## Skills¥n14¥t¥n15¥t- **archon-lean4** ¥u2014 installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.¥n16¥t¥n17¥t## Tools¥n18¥t¥n19¥tProject tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.¥n20¥t¥n21¥tTwo always-present scripts:¥n22¥t¥n23¥t- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** ¥u2014 call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) ¥u2014 check via `env | grep -E ¥

` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog ¥u2014 when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there ¥u2014 or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.¥n24¥t- **`archon-subagent.py`** ¥u2014 the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent ¥u2014 see ¥

below).¥n25¥t¥n26¥t## Subagents¥n27¥t¥n28¥tSubagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter ¥u2014 `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` ¥u2014 followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).¥n29¥t¥n30¥t**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.¥n31¥t¥n32¥t**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):¥n33¥t¥n34¥t```¥n35¥tpython3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py ¥¥¥n36¥t  --name <subagent-name> ¥¥¥n37¥t  --slug <kebab-slug> ¥¥¥n38¥t  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> ¥¥¥n39¥t  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple¥n40¥t```¥n41¥t¥n42¥tThe wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.¥n43¥t¥n44¥t**Treat each dispatch as blocking ¥u2014 wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately ¥u2014 that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.¥n45¥t¥n46¥t**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip ¥u2014 when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions ¥u2014 read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors ¥u2014 its semantic meaning is ¥
, not ¥

.)¥n47¥t¥n48¥t## Key Files & Permissions¥n49¥t¥n50¥tAll Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row ¥u2014 they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See ¥

after the table.¥n51¥t¥n52¥t| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |¥n53¥t|------|------|--------|--------|------|¥n54¥t| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |¥n55¥t| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | ¥u2014 | ¥u2014 | read |¥n56¥t| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | ¥u2014 | ¥u2014 | write |¥n57¥t| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |¥n58¥t| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |¥n59¥t| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |¥n60¥t| `proof-journal/` | read | ¥u2014 | **write** | read |¥n61¥t| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | ¥u2014 | **write** (Knowledge Base only ¥u2014 session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |¥n62¥t| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | ¥u2014 | read (recent window injected) | read |¥n63¥t| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | ¥u2014 | **write** (this iter only) | read |¥n64¥t| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | ¥u2014 | read | read |¥n65¥t| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |¥n66¥t| `.lean` files | ¥u2014 | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | ¥u2014 | write (via comments) |¥n67¥t| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `¥¥lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `¥¥mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `¥¥lean{...}` corrections ¥u2014 NOT `¥¥leanok`) | read |¥n68¥t| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | ¥u2014 | ¥u2014 | read |¥n69¥t| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |¥n70¥t¥n71¥t### Subagent permissions¥n72¥t¥n73¥tEach subagent ships YAML frontmatter:¥n74¥t¥n75¥t- `write_domain: ¥

` ¥u2014 file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).¥n76¥t- `read_only: true | false` ¥u2014 when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.¥n77¥t- `can_spawn: true | false` ¥u2014 when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.¥n78¥t¥n79¥tThe dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).¥n80¥t¥n81¥t**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow ¥u2014 treat those as historical noise.¥n82¥t¥n83¥t## Protected Declarations¥n84¥t¥n85¥t`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:¥n86¥t¥n87¥t- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.¥n88¥t- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.¥n89¥t- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.¥n90¥t¥n91¥t## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary¥n92¥t¥n93¥tTwo semantic markers:¥n94¥t¥n95¥t- `¥¥leanok` ¥u2014 statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.¥n96¥t- `¥¥mathlibok` ¥u2014 statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.¥n97¥t¥n98¥tNo marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.¥n99¥t¥n100¥t**`¥¥leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `¥¥lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `¥¥leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `¥¥leanok` themselves.¥n101¥t¥n102¥t`¥¥mathlibok`, `¥¥lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `¥¥lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.¥n103¥t¥n104¥t## User Interaction¥n105¥t¥n106¥tThe user provides hints in two places:¥n107¥t¥n108¥t- **Strategic hints** ¥u2192 `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as ¥

to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.¥n109¥t- **File-specific hints** ¥u2192 `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.¥n110¥t¥n111¥tArchon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** ¥u2014 a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:¥n112¥t¥n113¥t- **Concise**: the whole file stays ¥u2264 2¥u20133 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.¥n114¥t- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.¥n115¥t- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write ¥
, an options menu, a ¥
, or ¥

¥u2014 anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.¥n116¥t¥n117¥t## Agent Roles¥n118¥t¥n119¥t### Plan Agent¥n120¥t¥n121¥t- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.¥n122¥t- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not ¥

those files.¥n123¥t- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.¥n124¥t- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.¥n125¥t- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.¥n126¥t- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers ¥u2014 `¥¥leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `¥¥mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.¥n127¥t- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.¥n128¥t¥n129¥t### Prover Agent¥n130¥t¥n131¥t- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).¥n132¥t- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt ¥u2014 read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.¥n133¥t- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.¥n134¥t- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.¥n135¥t- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned ¥u2014 never edit another agent's file.¥n136¥t- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.¥n137¥t- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.¥n138¥t¥n139¥t### Subagents¥n140¥t¥n141¥tSubagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see ¥

above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at ¥u2014 never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state ¥u2014 and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).¥n142¥t¥n143¥t### Review Agent¥n144¥t¥n145¥t- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.¥n146¥t- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.¥n147¥t- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).¥n148¥t- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only ¥u2014 session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).¥n149¥t- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers ¥u2014 `¥¥mathlibok`, `¥¥lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `¥¥notready` removal. Do NOT touch `¥¥leanok`.¥n150¥t- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.¥n151¥t¥n152¥t### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)¥n153¥t¥n154¥tThese run automatically between phases:¥n155¥t¥n156¥t- **Iter sidecar init** ¥u2014 creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.¥n157¥t- **`sync_leanok`** ¥u2014 runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `¥¥leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.¥n158¥t- **`blueprint-doctor`** ¥u2014 runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `¥¥ref` / `¥¥uses` / `¥¥proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.¥n159¥t

1¥t# DAG Elaboration Agent¥n2¥t¥n3¥tYou are the DAG elaboration agent. Your mission: produce a mathematically complete, dependency-correct informal blueprint for the **entire project** ¥u2014 the full mathematical roadmap that `archon loop` will follow to produce formal Lean proofs.¥n4¥t¥n5¥t## What ¥

means¥n6¥t¥n7¥tA blueprint is **complete** when:¥n8¥t¥n9¥t1. Every declaration the project needs to prove has a blueprint entry (`¥¥begin{definition}`, `¥¥begin{lemma}`, `¥¥begin{theorem}`, etc.) with a `¥¥label{}` and `¥¥lean{}` annotation.¥n10¥t2. Every `¥¥uses{}` reference points to a label that exists somewhere in the blueprint (no broken refs).¥n11¥t3. Every declaration that logically depends on another declares that dependency via `¥¥uses{}`.¥n12¥t4. Every externally-sourced declaration has a `% SOURCE:` / `% SOURCE QUOTE:` citation block backed by a local file under `references/`.¥n13¥t5. `blueprint/src/content.tex` `¥¥input{}`'s every chapter file.¥n14¥t6. **No node has effort = ¥u221e.** Every declaration's proof closure is finite: each declaration either has an informal proof written, or is already proved sorry-free in Lean (in which case its blueprint entry carries a short ¥

note). The `leandag` tool is what tells you where the ¥u221e holes are ¥u2014 see below.¥n15¥t7. **Every statement and proof is purely mathematical prose ¥u2014 no Lean code inside.** The only Lean reference in a blueprint entry is the `¥¥lean{}` annotation that names the declaration; the body is mathematics, never Lean syntax. However, formalization might require more specific statements, definitions, or lemmas, and proofs would also require some details beyond a high-level sketch. Therefore, while the blueprint must be purely mathematical, it should still account for the needs of the formalization. ¥n16¥t¥n17¥t> Remark: Since the goal will eventually be formalization of the blueprint, the best practice is to rely on existing mathlib infrastructure the most, choosing the routes that rely the most on them. To show explicitly the mathlib dependencies, you can write latex statements corresponding to mathlib statements and label them with `¥¥mathlibok`, this way the dag considers them as ¥

and it stays clear whether it relies a lot on mathlib or not.¥n18¥t¥n19¥t## External references¥n20¥t¥n21¥tThe mathematics in the blueprint need to be grounded in **external sources**, avoid your training memory. A blueprint built on half-remembered statements is *worse than no blueprint at all*, because the prover loop will faithfully formalize whatever you wrote ¥u2014 including a subtly wrong statement that then costs enormous effort downstream. Treat every external dependency as load-bearing.¥n22¥t¥n23¥t- Every declaration block derived from external material **must** carry the `% SOURCE:` / `% SOURCE QUOTE:` / `¥¥textit{Source: ...}` citation block, backed **verbatim** by a local file under `references/` (see DAG integrity rule 6 and the blueprint-writer descriptor).¥n24¥t- When a chapter needs source material the project doesn't yet have, dispatch a **`reference-retriever` first**, then write the block against the retrieved file. Never let a writer paper over a missing source with a paraphrase from memory.¥n25¥t¥n26¥t### When a reference cannot be obtained¥n27¥t¥n28¥tSome sources are genuinely unreachable: paywalled, offline, behind an API key you don't have, or simply not available online. When a `reference-retriever` fails to obtain something you can write in `TO_USER.md` to ask the user to supply it, but it might not be able to retrieve it either. If no alternative source is available, you still need to write the blueprint, but you should mention `¥¥textit{Source: [no reference available]}` to keep the provenance clear. If statements are based on existing source, while it is much better to use the original statement, if you modified it, you should mention it in `¥¥textit{Source: ...}` as well, for transparency.¥n29¥t¥n30¥t## What you DO NOT do¥n31¥t¥n32¥t- You do NOT write or edit `.lean` files.¥n33¥t- You do NOT fill proofs in Lean.¥n34¥t- You do NOT add `¥¥leanok` markers ¥u2014 those are earned by a sorry-free Lean proof and set by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase. (`¥¥mathlibok` is different: you and your writers MAY mark it on explicit Mathlib dependency anchors, per the remark above.)¥n35¥t- You do NOT run `lake build` or any Lean compilation.¥n36¥t- You **own and maintain `.archon/STRATEGY.md`** (the long-arc strategy the blueprint serves ¥u2014 see ¥

below). You run before `archon loop`, so you establish it and the loop's planner continues it.¥n37¥t- You **maintain `.archon/PROGRESS.md`'s objectives** so they stay consistent with the strategy and blueprint you produce ¥u2014 while blueprinting you may arrive at better objectives than the initial ones, and the loop's planner picks up from what you leave here. You do NOT write `task_pending.md` or `task_done.md` (the prover task queue), and you do NOT fabricate prover-execution state inside PROGRESS.md (build status, `¥¥leanok`, attempt history) ¥u2014 that part is the loop's.¥n38¥t¥n39¥t## Understanding the project scope¥n40¥t¥n41¥tBefore writing any blueprint content, read:¥n42¥t¥n43¥t1. **Your invocation prompt** ¥u2014 it contains injected blocks: the Lean file list, existing chapters, goal description (if present), references summary, blueprint-doctor findings, and prior iter sidecars.¥n44¥t2. **`.lean` files** (if lean_aware) ¥u2014 read them to extract declaration signatures. Look for `sorry` stubs and docstrings that describe what each declaration should say mathematically.¥n45¥t3. **`PROJECT_GOAL.md` / `ARCHON_GOAL.md`** (if present) ¥u2014 high-level description of what the project is formalizing.¥n46¥t4. **`.archon/STRATEGY.md`** (if present) ¥u2014 the long-arc strategy. Read it early; you maintain it (see ¥

). The blueprint you build must serve this strategy, and the strategy must reflect the routes the blueprint takes ¥u2014 keep the two consistent.¥n47¥t5. **`.archon/PROGRESS.md`** (if present) ¥u2014 the current prover objectives and per-file state. Read it for alignment (don't re-blueprint what's already done), and **keep its objectives consistent with the strategy and blueprint you produce** ¥u2014 if your work yields a better plan, update the objectives here too (see ¥

). Don't overwrite or fabricate prover-execution state you can't know.¥n48¥t6. **`references/`** ¥u2014 the project's source material. Read `references/summary.md` first, then the relevant source files for declarations you're about to blueprint.¥n49¥t7. **Existing blueprint chapters** ¥u2014 understand what is already there before writing more.¥n50¥t¥n51¥t## Planning the chapter structure¥n52¥t¥n53¥tThe blueprint chapter structure mirrors the Lean file structure:¥n54¥t¥n55¥t- `Foo/Bar.lean` ¥u2192 `blueprint/src/chapters/Foo_Bar.tex`¥n56¥t- When several related Lean files belong to one mathematical topic, use a **consolidated chapter** by declaring at the top: `% archon:covers Foo/Bar.lean Foo/Baz.lean`¥n57¥t¥n58¥tDecide the chapter structure before dispatching writers. Group declarations mathematically, not mechanically. A consolidated chapter that covers 3¥u20135 tightly related files is better than 5 thin chapters that just forward to it.¥n59¥t¥n60¥t## Long-arc strategy (`.archon/STRATEGY.md`)¥n61¥t¥n62¥tYou function like the loop's plan agent for strategy: **`.archon/STRATEGY.md` is the living arc of how the project gets from the current state to ¥

**, and you establish and maintain it so the loop's planner can continue it. The blueprint is the *roadmap*; STRATEGY.md is the *plan that chose the roadmap's routes*. Keep them consistent ¥u2014 every route the strategy names must have blueprint coverage, and every chapter you write must serve a phase the strategy lists.¥n63¥t¥n64¥t- **Read it early**; update it when the strategy itself changes (route swap, phase split/merge, a new Mathlib gap discovered, a resolved/new strategic question). Follow the same canonical skeleton the plan agent uses ¥u2014 it is documented in `.archon/prompts/plan.md` under its ¥

section; read that for the exact headings/format when creating or restructuring STRATEGY.md. Keep the whole file human-readable and bounded (‾250 lines).¥n65¥t- Keep the goal, `STRATEGY.md`, the blueprint, and `PROGRESS.md`'s objectives **mutually consistent**. While blueprinting you will often arrive at a *better plan and better objectives than the initial ones* ¥u2014 when you do, propagate the change across all of them: update `STRATEGY.md` (the arc), the blueprint (the roadmap), and `PROGRESS.md`'s `## Current Objectives` (what the loop picks up next). Reconcile; don't let them drift. The only part of `PROGRESS.md` that is off-limits is the prover-execution state (build status, `¥¥leanok`, attempt history) ¥u2014 the loop fills that. The objective format is documented in `.archon/prompts/plan.md`; match it (or the existing PROGRESS.md) so the loop continues seamlessly.¥n66¥t- **Have it reviewed.** After you establish or change STRATEGY.md, dispatch **`strategy-critic`** (in your catalog) to render a fresh-context verdict on whether the strategy is sound and well-formatted, exactly as the plan agent does. Act on its verdict before declaring the iteration's work done.¥n67¥t¥n68¥t## Your workflow each iteration¥n69¥t¥n70¥t### Step 1 ¥u2014 Assess the current state¥n71¥t¥n72¥tRead the injected context: existing chapters, blueprint-doctor findings, frontier summary. If prior iter sidecars are present, understand what gaps remain. Then **run `leandag` to ground that assessment in the real DAG** ¥u2014 `leandag build --html` to refresh the graph, then `leandag stats` and `leandag focus` to see the effort accounting, the remaining ¥u221e-nodes, and the ranked agenda. Trust `leandag` over your recollection of where things stand.¥n73¥t¥n74¥t### Step 2 ¥u2014 Plan the chapter dispatches¥n75¥t¥n76¥tFor each chapter that needs to be written or substantially extended, prepare a blueprint-writer directive. Use `leandag focus` / `leandag show gaps` / `leandag query --chapter <c>` to decide *which* chapters to dispatch and *which declarations* each must cover. Each directive must name:¥n77¥t- The chapter slug and target `.tex` file.¥n78¥t- The declarations that must appear (with enough mathematical content to be useful).¥n79¥t- **The relevant references ¥u2014 the exact `references/<file>.md` paths the writer must cite verbatim.** If a needed source is not yet in `references/`, plan a `reference-retriever` first (Step 3). A directive with a vague or empty references section invites fabrication; name the sources.¥n80¥t- The scope boundary (what NOT to include).¥n81¥t¥n82¥t### Step 3 ¥u2014 Dispatch subagents¥n83¥t¥n84¥tUse the subagents in your catalog:¥n85¥t¥n86¥t- **`blueprint-writer`** ¥u2014 dispatch one per chapter that needs writing/extending. Give precise directives. Writers must follow citation discipline (see their descriptor at `.archon/subagents/blueprint-writer.md`). NEVER instruct a writer to add `¥¥leanok` markers.¥n87¥t- **`blueprint-reviewer`** ¥u2014 dispatch after writers complete, to audit the whole blueprint for completeness and correctness. It also runs `leandag` to audit the dependency graph and reports a **`### Dependency & isolation findings`** section: each broken/missing `¥¥uses{}` and each isolated node tagged `wire-up`, `remove`, or `keep`. Turn each `wire-up`/`remove` into a follow-up blueprint-writer directive scoped to that node's chapter. **Removal is gated:** a writer deletes an isolated block only when your directive explicitly authorizes it ¥u2014 so only authorize `remove` after you've confirmed the reviewer's call (it's not the goal, not a `¥¥mathlibok` anchor, and nothing in the goal's closure needs it). When in doubt, prefer `wire-up` (add the missing edge) over deletion.¥n88¥t- **`reference-retriever`** ¥u2014 dispatch when a chapter needs source material not yet in `references/`. Can also be dispatched by blueprint-writers mid-session when they discover a missing source.¥n89¥t- **`strategy-critic`** ¥u2014 dispatch after you establish or change `.archon/STRATEGY.md` (see ¥

), before finishing the iteration. It reads STRATEGY.md with fresh context and renders a verdict on whether the strategy is sound and matches its canonical skeleton. Act on its verdict.¥n90¥t¥n91¥tThe dispatcher pattern:¥n92¥t```¥n93¥tpython3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py ¥¥¥n94¥t  --name blueprint-writer ¥¥¥n95¥t  --slug <chapter-slug> ¥¥¥n96¥t  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/dag-writer-<slug>-directive.md ¥¥¥n97¥t  --write-domain 'blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter>.tex' ¥¥¥n98¥t  --write-domain 'references/**'¥n99¥t```¥n100¥t¥n101¥tWrite the directive file first (see `.archon/subagents/blueprint-writer.md` for directive format), then dispatch.¥n102¥t¥n103¥t### Step 4 ¥u2014 Update content.tex¥n104¥t¥n105¥tAfter writers complete, ensure `blueprint/src/content.tex` `¥¥input{}`'s every chapter:¥n106¥t```latex¥n107¥t¥¥input{chapters/<slug>}¥n108¥t```¥n109¥t¥n110¥t> You may also use `leanblueprint web` to ensure that there is no mismatch when the blueprint is rendered, and `leandag build --html` to confirm the updated chapters parse cleanly into the DAG (watch the build report for unmatched `¥¥lean{}` and unknown `¥¥uses{}`).¥n111¥t¥n112¥t### Step 5 ¥u2014 Declare status¥n113¥t¥n114¥tAfter all writers and the reviewer have run, assess completeness. **Re-run `leandag` first** ¥u2014 `leandag build` then `leandag stats` and `leandag focus` ¥u2014 and let it decide. You may only declare COMPLETE when: `leandag stats` reports **zero ¥u221e-nodes**, `leandag focus` shows an **empty `unmatched_lean`** (every Lean decl has a blueprint entry), and `leandag show gaps` is **empty** (every blueprint decl has a `¥¥lean{}`).¥n115¥t¥n116¥t- If the blueprint covers the full scope (all declarations present, no broken `¥¥uses{}` refs, no ¥u221e-nodes per `leandag` and the reviewer's report):¥n117¥t  ```markdown¥n118¥t  ## Status: COMPLETE¥n119¥t  ¥n120¥t  ## Declared coverage¥n121¥t  - blueprint/src/chapters/Foo_Bar.tex ¥u2014 covers Foo/Bar.lean: <list of declarations>¥n122¥t  - ...¥n123¥t  ```¥n124¥t  Write this to `.archon/DAG_STATUS.md`. The loop stops when it sees `## Status: COMPLETE`.¥n125¥t¥n126¥t- If gaps remain:¥n127¥t  ```markdown¥n128¥t  ## Status: in_progress¥n129¥t  ¥n130¥t  ## Iterations completed: N¥n131¥t  ¥n132¥t  ## Remaining gaps¥n133¥t  - <list of undone declarations or broken deps>¥n134¥t  ¥n135¥t  ## Declared coverage so far¥n136¥t  - ...¥n137¥t  ```¥n138¥t¥n139¥t### Step 6 ¥u2014 Write your narrative¥n140¥t¥n141¥tWrite a concise narrative to `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/dag.md` explaining:¥n142¥t- What chapters you dispatched writers for.¥n143¥t- What the blueprint-reviewer found.¥n144¥t- The `leandag stats` picture (effort done/remaining, ¥u221e-node count) before vs. after this iteration.¥n145¥t- What remains ¥u2014 and, prominently, **any external reference you could not obtain**: what you need and which declarations are affected. Mirror these into `TO_USER.md` (see ¥

) so the user has one place to act on them.¥n146¥t¥n147¥t## The leandag tool ¥u2014 your source of truth for dependencies and effort¥n148¥t¥n149¥t`leandag` parses the actual `.lean` files and the blueprint LaTeX, builds the¥n150¥treal dependency DAG of every declaration, and computes formalization-effort¥n151¥tmetrics. **Do not rely on your own intuition about the dependency structure or¥n152¥tabout what is missing ¥u2014 it is frequently wrong.** `leandag` is the only thing¥n153¥tthat knows where the holes are; consult it constantly and **use it¥n154¥tsubstantially throughout every iteration.**¥n155¥t¥n156¥tDrive it through the **`leandag` CLI** ¥u2014 the one interface for querying the DAG.¥n157¥t`leandag` is installed on the same PATH as `archon`, so you can run it directly¥n158¥tfrom Bash, many times per iteration:¥n159¥t¥n160¥t| Command | When and why you run it |¥n161¥t|---------|-------------------------|¥n162¥t| `leandag build --html` | **First thing each iteration, and after every writer batch.** Re-parses every `.lean` file and blueprint chapter into the DAG, caches `.leandag/dag.json` (every other command reads this cache, so build first), and with `--html` regenerates `.leandag/graph.html` ¥u2014 the interactive dependency graph. |¥n163¥t| `leandag stats` | Project overview: declaration counts, proved %, ready/gaps/unmatched, and the **effort accounting** (`effort_done`, `effort_remaining`, and the count of ¥u221e-nodes). This is your headline progress number. |¥n164¥t| `leandag focus` | **Your agenda ¥u2014 what to work on next.** Ranks `ready_to_formalize` (by impact), `has_sorry`, `needs_lean_statement` (blueprint statements missing `¥¥lean{}`), `needs_leanok`, and `unmatched_lean`. Re-run it to choose the next batch of writer dispatches. |¥n165¥t| `leandag show <what>` | A named subset: `axioms`, `leaves`, `isolated`, `unproved`, `sorry`, `ready`, `gaps`, `leanok`. `show gaps` lists blueprint declarations still missing a `¥¥lean{}` statement (DAG integrity rule 1). `show isolated` (also `leandag query --isolated`, and the `isolated`/`isolated_blueprint` counts in `leandag stats`) lists declarations with no edges in or out ¥u2014 usually a *missing `¥¥uses{}` edge* to fix, occasionally orphaned scaffolding to remove. For the reverse of `gaps` ¥u2014 Lean decls with *no* blueprint entry ¥u2014 use `leandag focus` (its `unmatched_lean` list). |¥n166¥t| `leandag query ...` | Arbitrary slice: `--sort effort¥¥|deps¥¥|impact¥¥|id`, `--unproved`, `--max-deps`, `--min-deps`, `--min-effort`, `--max-effort`, `--chapter`, `--type`, `--top`. E.g. `leandag query --unproved --sort impact --top 20` surfaces the biggest blockers ¥u2014 the nodes that, once written, unblock the most descendants. |¥n167¥t¥n168¥tOutput format is selectable per call: append `--format json` (or `-f json`, or¥n169¥tthe per-command `--json`) for machine-readable data you can reason over¥n170¥tdirectly, or `--plain` for plain text. In json/text mode the structured payload¥n171¥tgoes to **stdout** and progress lines to **stderr**, so you read clean data.¥n172¥t¥n173¥t**Use it substantially ¥u2014 a concrete cadence:**¥n174¥t¥n175¥t- **Start of iteration:** `leandag build --html`, then `leandag stats` and¥n176¥t  `leandag focus` to take stock and plan the iteration's dispatches.¥n177¥t- **Scoping each writer:** `leandag show gaps` / `leandag query --chapter <c>`¥n178¥t  to see exactly which declarations that chapter must cover and what they depend¥n179¥t  on ¥u2014 feed this into the writer's directive.¥n180¥t- **After writers return:** `leandag build` again, then `leandag stats` /¥n181¥t  `leandag focus` to confirm the gap actually closed (don't assume it did).¥n182¥t- **Before `## Status: COMPLETE`:** `leandag stats` must show zero ¥u221e-nodes and¥n183¥t  `leandag show gaps` must be empty.¥n184¥t¥n185¥t### The injected coverage summary¥n186¥t¥n187¥tA coverage/infinity summary is already injected into your prompt each iteration¥n188¥tunder `## Blueprint coverage (leandag)` ¥u2014 **read it first**, then re-query the¥n189¥tlive DAG with the `leandag` CLI above (it is the only tool you invoke for this).¥n190¥tThe summary lists:¥n191¥t¥n192¥t- **Uncovered Lean declarations** ¥u2014 `.lean` decls with no blueprint entry (no¥n193¥t  `¥¥lean{}` points at them; the `unmatched_lean` list in `leandag focus`). Each¥n194¥t  needs a blueprint entry: dispatch a `blueprint-writer` for the chapter that¥n195¥t  should cover it.¥n196¥t- **Broken `¥¥uses{}` references** ¥u2014 a `¥¥uses{}` label no declaration defines.¥n197¥t  Fix by adding the missing declaration or correcting the label.¥n198¥t- **Infinity sources** ¥u2014 the declarations that make the roadmap incomplete (see¥n199¥t  below). Ordered closest-to-the-root first. **This is your priority work list.**¥n200¥t- **Proved in Lean but no informal proof** ¥u2014 sorry-free in Lean but the¥n201¥t  blueprint body is empty. Add a one-line ¥
note so the¥n202¥t  entry isn't mistaken for an ¥u221e hole.¥n203¥t- **Unproved / ready** ¥u2014 informational; the prover loop consumes these later.¥n204¥t¥n205¥t### The effort model (what ¥

means and why you must kill it)¥n206¥t¥n207¥t`leandag` assigns each declaration an **effort**:¥n208¥t¥n209¥t- `effort_local = 0` ¥u2014 the declaration is **proved sorry-free in Lean**. Done.¥n210¥t- `effort_local = <number>` ¥u2014 **no Lean proof yet, but an informal proof is¥n211¥t  written** (the number is its size). Fine for the blueprint ¥u2014 the loop will¥n212¥t  formalize it.¥n213¥t- `effort_local = ¥u221e` ¥u2014 **neither**: no informal proof AND no sorry-free Lean.¥n214¥t  This is a hole.¥n215¥t- `effort_total` sums `effort_local` over the declaration **and all its¥n216¥t  ancestors**. If any ancestor is ¥u221e, the whole closure is ¥u221e.¥n217¥t¥n218¥t**Your objective: no declaration may have effort = ¥u221e.** A finite effort¥n219¥teverywhere means the blueprint is a complete mathematical roadmap ¥u2014 every step¥n220¥teither has a written proof or is already done in Lean.¥n221¥t¥n222¥t### How to eliminate ¥u221e (work from the root)¥n223¥t¥n224¥t1. Take the **Infinity sources** list (already ordered closest-to-root first).¥n225¥t2. For the root-most source: if its proof draws on external material, dispatch a¥n226¥t   `reference-retriever`, then **write its informal LaTeX proof** (via a¥n227¥t   `blueprint-writer`). Writing a source's proof shrinks every ¥u221e that depended¥n228¥t   on it ¥u2014 that's why you start at the root.¥n229¥t3. If a source is in the **¥

** list, you¥n230¥t   cannot (and need not) write the maths ¥u2014 you lack Lean write permission ¥u2014 but¥n231¥t   add a short note like `¥¥begin{proof} Proved directly in Lean. ¥¥end{proof}` so¥n232¥t   the entry is recognized as finished rather than an ¥u221e hole.¥n233¥t4. Re-run the tool and repeat until the **Infinity sources** list is empty.¥n234¥t¥n235¥t## Write permissions¥n236¥t¥n237¥tYou may write:¥n238¥t- `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` ¥u2014 all chapters¥n239¥t- `blueprint/src/content.tex` ¥u2014 chapter index¥n240¥t- `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex` ¥u2014 shared macros¥n241¥t- `.archon/STRATEGY.md` ¥u2014 the long-arc strategy (you establish it; the loop's planner continues it)¥n242¥t- `.archon/PROGRESS.md` ¥u2014 the objectives/plan: keep `## Current Objectives` consistent with STRATEGY.md + the blueprint (the loop continues it). Do NOT fabricate prover-execution state (build status, `¥¥leanok`, attempt history).¥n243¥t- `.archon/DAG_STATUS.md` ¥u2014 completion status¥n244¥t- `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/dag.md` ¥u2014 iteration narrative¥n245¥t- `TO_USER.md` ¥u2014 requests for the user (e.g. unreachable references to supply)¥n246¥t- Directive files for subagents under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/`¥n247¥t¥n248¥tYou must NOT write:¥n249¥t- `.lean` files¥n250¥t- `.archon/task_pending.md`, `.archon/task_done.md` (the prover task queue)¥n251¥t- `.archon/task_results/` files¥n252¥t¥n253¥t## DAG integrity rules¥n254¥t¥n255¥tThese rules ensure the blueprint works as a mathematical roadmap:¥n256¥t¥n257¥t1. **Every declaration has `¥¥lean{}`**: The name must match the Lean declaration, including namespace. Use a placeholder like `¥¥lean{Project.TODO.theorem_name}` when the exact Lean name is not yet known ¥u2014 better than omitting.¥n258¥t¥n259¥t2. **`¥¥uses{}` must be complete**: If theorem T's proof uses lemma L, then T's blueprint entry must declare `¥¥uses{lem:L}`. Missing deps cause the main loop to dispatch provers out of order.¥n260¥t¥n261¥t3. **`¥¥uses{}` must be accurate**: Every label in `¥¥uses{}` must be defined in some chapter. The blueprint-doctor flags broken refs after you're done.¥n262¥t¥n263¥t4. **Mathlib-provided results need no chapter**: Definitions and lemmas that the project imports from Mathlib without modification do NOT need their own blueprint chapter. Two ways to record the dependency: reference them via `¥¥uses{}` (the review agent marks them `¥¥mathlibok`), or ¥u2014 often better for a load-bearing dependency ¥u2014 have a writer author an explicit **Mathlib dependency anchor**: a block that states the Mathlib result, points `¥¥lean{}` at the real Mathlib declaration, and is marked `¥¥mathlibok`. The explicit anchor resolves the `¥¥uses{}` to a real (done) node and makes the route's reliance on Mathlib visible. Either way, a `¥¥mathlibok` claim must be faithful to an actual Mathlib declaration ¥u2014 the reviewer audits this.¥n264¥t¥n265¥t5. **`content.tex` must be complete**: Every chapter under `blueprint/src/chapters/` must be `¥¥input{}`'d by `content.tex`. The blueprint-doctor flags orphan chapters.¥n266¥t¥n267¥t6. **Citation discipline (the hard rule)**: Every declaration block derived from external material requires `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, and `¥¥textit{Source: ...}` lines. Writers enforce this rule ¥u2014 your directives must give them the relevant `references/<file>.md` paths. Never fabricate citations.¥n268¥t¥n269¥t7. **Purely mathematical, no Lean code**: Statements and proofs are ordinary mathematical prose and LaTeX. Never put Lean syntax, tactic blocks, or code fences inside a statement or proof body ¥u2014 the only Lean reference is the `¥¥lean{}` annotation naming the declaration. Keep entries clear and concise: a precise statement and a readable proof sketch, not a wall of text.¥n270¥t¥n271¥t## Reading Lean files (lean-aware mode)¥n272¥t¥n273¥tWhen `.lean` files are listed in your injected context, read the ones that are relevant to the chapter you're planning. You are looking for:¥n274¥t¥n275¥t- Declaration signatures (`def`, `theorem`, `lemma`, `instance`, `class`, `structure`) ¥u2014 even if the body is `sorry`.¥n276¥t- Docstrings (`/-- ... -/`) above declarations ¥u2014 these often describe the mathematical intent.¥n277¥t- Module namespace and imports ¥u2014 these tell you the dependency structure at the Lean level.¥n278¥t¥n279¥tYou read `.lean` files; you never edit them.¥n280¥t¥n281¥t## When the blueprint already exists¥n282¥t¥n283¥tIf prior iterations produced blueprint chapters (listed in your injected context), do NOT recreate them from scratch. Instead:¥n284¥t¥n285¥t1. Read existing chapters to understand what's already there.¥n286¥t2. Identify gaps: missing declarations, broken `¥¥uses{}`, chapters with no `¥¥lean{}`, declarations without proof sketches.¥n287¥t3. Dispatch writers scoped to the gaps, not the whole chapter.¥n288¥t4. Merge the reviewer's findings with your gap analysis to decide dispatch scope.¥n289¥t¥n290¥t## Project types and how to scope the blueprint¥n291¥t¥n292¥tDepending on the project, the user's goal might be in a `.md` file, in `.lean` directly (completing the sorries), in the existing blueprints, in the references, or some combination. The blueprints should therefore all of the dependencies that are necessary to reach the goal.
Let me assess the current state by reading the strategy, progress, status, and prior narrative files.

1¥t# Strategy¥n2¥t¥n3¥t## Goal¥n4¥t¥n5¥tFormalize Christian Merten's Jacobian challenge (`references/challenge.lean.ref`): the nine¥n6¥tprotected declarations headed by `AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian` and¥n7¥t`Jacobian.nonempty_jacobianWitness` ¥u2014 an Albanese/Jacobian object uniform over the `k`-rational¥n8¥tpointing of a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve `C/k` (`[Field k]` only; no `C(k)¥u2260¥u2205`,¥n9¥tno `CharZero`). `J := Pic¥u2070_{C/k}` is built unconditionally; only `isAlbaneseFor` is quantified over¥n10¥tthe pointing. The combined proof,¥n% sharing the ¥¥texttt{¥¥% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:} comment, follows the third¥n% block.¥n¥n¥¥begin{theorem}¥¥leanok¥n[¥¥(¥¥Pic^0_{C/k}¥¥) is an abelian variety]¥n  ¥¥label{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}¥n  ¥¥lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.isAbelianVariety}¥n¥n  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', ¥¥S 5, Ex.‾ex:jac + Rmk.‾rmk:Jac;¥n  % arXiv:math/0504020, pp.‾50--51 (read from¥n  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3911-L3917 +¥n  % L3990-L3996).¥n  % SOURCE QUOTE:¥n  % ¥
¥n  % SOURCE QUOTE:¥n  % ¥

¥n  ¥¥textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', ¥¥S 5,¥n  Ex.‾ex:jac + Rmk.‾rmk:Jac; cf.¥¥ [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'',¥n  ¥¥S I.1 (def.¥¥ of abelian variety, p.‾8).}¥n  Let ¥¥(C/k¥¥) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus¥n  ¥¥(g¥¥) over a field ¥¥(k¥¥). The identity component ¥¥(¥¥Pic^0_{C/k}¥¥) of¥n  ¥¥cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme} is an ¥¥emph{abelian variety over¥n  ¥¥(k¥¥)}: it is proper, smooth, and geometrically irreducible as a¥n  ¥¥(k¥¥)-scheme, and it carries a ¥¥(k¥¥)-group-scheme structure. (In the¥n  Lean encoding this is the conjunction of¥n  ¥¥texttt{[IsProper]}, ¥¥texttt{[Smooth]}, and¥n  ¥¥texttt{[GeometricallyIrreducible]} on the structural morphism¥n  ¥¥texttt{(Pic0Scheme C).hom} together with the existence of a¥n  ¥¥texttt{[GrpObj]} structure on ¥¥(¥¥Pic^0_{C/k}¥¥). Commutativity¥n  follows automatically from Milne‾¥¥S I.1, Cor.‾1.4 and is not¥n  separately stated.)¥n¥¥end{theorem}¥n¥n¥¥begin{theorem}¥n¥n¥¥leanok¥n[Dimension of ¥¥(¥¥Pic^0_{C/k}¥¥) equals the genus of ¥¥(C¥¥)]¥n  ¥¥label{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}¥n  ¥¥lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.finrank_eq_genus}¥n¥n  % SOURCE: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),¥n  % ¥¥S III.1, Rmk.‾1.4(e), p.‾86 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf,¥n  % PDF p.‾92).¥n  % SOURCE QUOTE:¥n  % ¥

¥n  ¥¥textit{Source: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),¥n  ¥¥S III.1, Rmk.‾1.4(e), p.‾86; cf.¥¥ [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'',¥n  ¥¥S 5, Cor.‾cor:sm + Ex.‾ex:jac.}¥n  Let ¥¥(C/k¥¥) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus¥n  ¥¥(g = g(C)¥¥) (¥¥cref{def:genus}) over a field ¥¥(k¥¥). The dimension of¥n  the abelian variety ¥¥(¥¥Pic^0_{C/k}¥¥)¥n  (¥¥cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}) equals the genus:¥n  ¥¥[¥n    ¥¥dim_k ¥¥Pic^0_{C/k} ¥¥;=¥¥; g(C).¥n  ¥¥]¥n¥¥end{theorem}¥n¥n=== IdentityComponent L615-700 (assembly proof) ===¥n% ¥

¥n¥¥begin{proof}¥n  We establish¥n  ¥¥cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety},¥n  ¥¥cref{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}, and¥n  ¥¥cref{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero} in turn, assembling¥n  the conclusions from the identity-component substrate of¥n  ¥¥cref{sec:identity_component_abstract} together with¥n  Kleiman‾¥¥S 5 Thm.‾th:qpp¥¥&p, Cor.‾cor:sm, and Ex.‾ex:jac, and¥n  Milne‾¥¥S I.1 Cor.‾1.4 (commutativity) + Rmk.‾III.1.4(e) (dimension).¥n¥n  ¥¥emph{Abelian-variety structure¥n  (¥¥cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}).} We assemble the four¥n  conjuncts from four inputs.¥n¥n  (i) ¥¥emph{Clopen subgroup of finite type, geometrically irreducible.}¥n  By¥n  ¥¥cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup,thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism,thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible,thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}¥n  applied to ¥¥(G = ¥¥Pic_{C/k}¥¥) (a ¥¥(k¥¥)-group scheme locally of finite¥n  type by¥n  ¥¥cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme,thm:fga_pic_representability}),¥n  ¥¥(¥¥Pic^0_{C/k}¥¥) is an open and closed subgroup scheme of ¥¥(¥¥Pic_{C/k}¥¥),¥n  of finite type over ¥¥(k¥¥), geometrically irreducible, and its¥n  formation commutes with extending ¥¥(k¥¥). In particular ¥¥(¥¥Pic^0_{C/k}¥¥)¥n  is separated and locally of finite type as a subscheme of¥n  ¥¥(¥¥Pic_{C/k}¥¥), and connected.¥n¥n  (ii) ¥¥emph{Quasi-projectivity.} The disjoint-union structure of¥n  ¥¥(¥¥Pic_{C/k}¥¥) (¥¥cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: a disjoint union¥n  of open quasi-projective ¥¥(k¥¥)-subschemes) restricted to ¥¥(¥¥Pic^0_{C/k}¥¥)¥n  expresses the identity component as a quasi-projective ¥¥(k¥¥)-scheme¥n  (Kleiman‾¥¥S 5, Thm.‾th:qpp¥¥&p: ¥¥(X/k¥¥) projective and geometrically¥n  integral ¥¥(¥¥Longrightarrow¥¥) ¥¥(¥¥Pic^0_{X/k}¥¥) is quasi-projective).¥n¥n  (iii) ¥¥emph{Projectivity (properness).} For a smooth proper¥n  geometrically integral curve ¥¥(C/k¥¥) of genus ¥¥(g > 0¥¥), ¥¥(C/k¥¥) is¥n  also geometrically normal (the smoothness implies geometric¥n  regularity, hence geometric normality). Kleiman‾¥¥S 5, Thm.‾th:qpp¥¥&p¥n  then upgrades the quasi-projective conclusion to projective:¥n  ¥¥(¥¥Pic^0_{C/k}¥¥) is projective over ¥¥(k¥¥), in particular proper.¥n  The proof of th:qpp¥¥&p uses the Chevalley--Rosenlicht structure¥n  theorem to reduce to ruling out non-constant maps¥n  ¥¥(¥¥mathbb G_m ¥¥to ¥¥Pic^0_{C/k}¥¥), which on a geometrically normal¥n  ¥¥(X¥¥) becomes a divisor-theoretic computation.¥n¥n  (iv) ¥¥emph{Smoothness.} For a smooth proper curve ¥¥(C/k¥¥), the¥n  Picard scheme is smooth at ¥¥(0¥¥): this is Exercise ex:jac of¥n  Kleiman‾¥¥S 5, which asserts that for ¥¥(X/S¥¥) projective and flat¥n  with integral geometric fibres of arithmetic genus ¥¥(p_a¥¥), the¥n  generalised Jacobians ¥¥(¥¥Pic^0_{X_s/k_s}¥¥) form a smooth¥n  quasi-projective family of relative dimension ¥¥(p_a¥¥); specialising¥n  to ¥¥(S = ¥¥Spec k¥¥) and ¥¥(X = C¥¥) a smooth proper curve of (geometric¥n  = arithmetic) genus ¥¥(g¥¥) gives smoothness of ¥¥(¥¥Pic^0_{C/k}¥¥).¥n¥n  Combining (i)--(iv): ¥¥(¥¥Pic^0_{C/k}¥¥) is a smooth, projective,¥n  geometrically irreducible ¥¥(k¥¥)-group scheme. A proper connected¥n  ¥¥(k¥¥)-group variety is by definition an ¥¥emph{abelian variety}¥n  (Milne‾¥¥S I.1, p.‾8), and is automatically commutative (Milne‾¥¥S I.1,¥n  Cor.‾1.4). This establishes¥n  ¥¥cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}.¥n¥n  ¥¥emph{Dimension equals the genus¥n  (¥¥cref{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}).} By Kleiman‾¥¥S 5,¥n  Cor.‾cor:sm, the inequality¥n  ¥¥(¥¥dim ¥¥Pic_{C/k} ¥¥le ¥¥dim_k H^1(C, ¥¥mathcal{O}_C)¥¥) holds, with¥n  equality if and only if ¥¥(¥¥Pic_{C/k}¥¥) is smooth at ¥¥(0¥¥), in which¥n  case ¥¥(¥¥Pic_{C/k}¥¥) is smooth of dimension ¥¥(¥¥dim_k H^1(C,¥n  ¥¥mathcal{O}_C)¥¥) everywhere. Step (iv) above showed¥n  ¥¥(¥¥Pic^0_{C/k}¥¥) is smooth at ¥¥(0¥¥); equality therefore holds on¥n  the open identity component, and¥n  ¥¥(¥¥dim_k ¥¥Pic^0_{C/k} = ¥¥dim_k H^1(C, ¥¥mathcal{O}_C) = g(C)¥¥)¥n  by ¥¥cref{def:genus}. This is Milne‾¥¥S III.1, Rmk.‾1.4(e): ``The¥n  dimension of ¥¥(J¥¥) is the genus of ¥¥(C¥¥)''.¥n¥n  ¥¥emph{Identification with the kernel of the degree map¥n  (¥¥cref{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero}).} A class¥n  ¥¥(¥¥lambda ¥¥in ¥¥Pic_{C/k}(k)¥¥) lies in the connected component of¥n  the identity --- i.e.¥¥ in the image of the open immersion¥n  ¥¥(¥¥Pic^0_{C/k} ¥¥hookrightarrow ¥¥Pic_{C/k}¥¥)¥n  (¥¥cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}) --- precisely when¥n  the Hilbert polynomial of a representing invertible sheaf coincides

Then:¥n  ¥¥begin{enumerate}[label=(¥¥roman*)]¥n    ¥¥item The set ¥¥(I¥¥) of Hilbert polynomials¥n      ¥¥(P_s(m) = ¥¥chi(¥¥PP^n_s, ¥¥F_s(m))¥¥) that arise as ¥¥(s¥¥) ranges over ¥¥(S¥¥) is¥n      finite.¥n    ¥¥item For each ¥¥(f ¥¥in I¥¥) there exists a locally-closed subscheme¥n      ¥¥(S_f ¥¥hookrightarrow S¥¥) whose underlying set ¥¥(|S_f|¥¥) consists of exactly¥n      those ¥¥(s ¥¥in S¥¥) with ¥¥(P_s = f¥¥).  The collection ¥¥(¥¥{|S_f|¥¥}_{f ¥¥in I}¥¥) is¥n      pairwise disjoint with set-theoretic union ¥¥(|S|¥¥).  Forming the coproduct¥n      ¥¥(S' := ¥¥coprod_{f ¥¥in I} S_f¥¥) and the morphism ¥¥(i : S' ¥¥to S¥¥) assembled¥n      from the inclusions ¥¥(S_f ¥¥hookrightarrow S¥¥), the pullback ¥¥(i^* ¥¥F¥¥) on¥n      ¥¥(¥¥PP^n_{S'}¥¥) is flat over ¥¥(S'¥¥), and ¥¥(i : S' ¥¥to S¥¥) is universal with this¥n      property: for any morphism ¥¥(¥¥varphi : T ¥¥to S¥¥) such that ¥¥(¥¥varphi^* ¥¥F¥¥) on¥n      ¥¥(¥¥PP^n_T¥¥) is flat over ¥¥(T¥¥), ¥¥(¥¥varphi¥¥) factors uniquely through ¥¥(i¥¥).¥n      Each ¥¥(S_f¥¥) is uniquely determined by ¥¥(f¥¥).¥n    ¥¥item Order ¥¥(I¥¥) by ¥¥(f < g ¥¥iff f(n) < g(n)¥¥) for all ¥¥(n ¥¥gg 0¥¥). Then the¥n      closure of ¥¥(|S_f|¥¥) in ¥¥(|S|¥¥) is contained in ¥¥(¥¥bigcup_{g ¥¥ge f} |S_g|¥¥).¥n  ¥¥end{enumerate}¥n¥¥end{theorem}¥n¥nThe proof is long; we split it into sub-lemmas below.
